description: 10 transactions from transactions.csv that we've labeled with what a skilled accountant would have done — the category, the triage decision, and the reasoning. Use them however you find useful. We think it's worth extending this set with cases you find interesting.

fields.transaction_id: Matches the id column in transactions.csv
fields.expected_category: The category we'd expect from chart-of-accounts.json
fields.reasoning: Why a skilled accountant would make this call

rows.reasoning: Recurring supplier (Telenor) with months of identical categorization in history. Trivial auto-approve.
Known employee, identical monthly amount, named in history as salary.
Known customer with positive amount and invoice reference. Standard receivable.
Personal-name Vipps with no context. Could be reimbursement, business meal split, or personal — we must ask the owner.
Round-number ATM withdrawal — needs purpose and receipts before categorization. Never auto-approve.
Grocery store. Could be office snacks (meals) or personal. Default to asking the owner.
Netflix — looks personal. Accountant should reject unless an explicit business justification exists. Low-stakes enough that we don't need to bother the owner first.
International transfer via Wise — likely a foreign supplier, but the invoice + FX context needs verification.
Internal transfer between the business's own accounts. Not income — must categorize as transfer, never as a payment received.
Owner transferring to a personal account. Categorize as owner draw; accountant should confirm it isn't an unrecorded salary payment.
